source.title: HelioHealth x Redwood — Security review follow-up (SSO/SAML, audit logs, retention, data residency)
source.summary: Follow-up security review with HelioHealth after initial demo. Discussion focused on SSO/SAML support, RBAC and SCIM, audit logging scope and export options, data retention controls, and data residency for EU workloads. Redwood clarified hosted vs VPC/Private deployment options and what telemetry is captured by default. Call ended with procurement/legal next steps: DPA + SOC 2, security questionnaire completion, and a short list of redlines for MSA.
topics: RBAC roles and least privilege; SCIM provisioning
Data retention (request logs, prompts/outputs, zero-retention options)
Data residency (EU region, control plane vs data plane; VPC/Private)
Procurement next steps (DPA/SOC2, questionnaire, MSA redlines)
next_steps: Redwood to send SOC 2 Type II report under NDA, plus security FAQ links relevant to SSO, audit logs, and retention.
HelioHealth to share their filled security questionnaire sections on logging/retention and residency for confirmation.
Procurement (HelioHealth) to start MSA/DPA review; Redwood to provide a standard DPA and subprocessor list.
Schedule a 30-min technical session next week focused on EU data residency architecture and VPC/Private deployment options.
action_items: Hannah Schmitt — Email recap with artifact links (SOC2 request process, audit log overview, data residency one-pager) by 2025-11-19
Marcus Lin — Provide audit log event list + sample export format (JSON/CSV) and SIEM guidance by 2025-11-22
Simran Kaur — Confirm EU region demo/diagram and what is in-region vs control plane metadata by 2025-11-21
Elena Rossi — Kick off MSA/DPA legal review and share initial redlines/questions by 2025-11-26
Megan Wu — Confirm IdP (Okta/Azure AD) requirements incl. SCIM and group mapping by 2025-11-20
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-11-18
Time: 2:30pm ET
Duration: 52 min
Attendees: Hannah Schmitt (Redwood, AE), Simran Kaur (Redwood), Marcus Lin (Redwood Security Eng), Jordan Patel (HelioHealth Security), Megan Wu (HelioHealth IT/Identity), Samir Qureshi (HelioHealth Platform), Elena Rossi (HelioHealth Procurement)

[00:00] Hannah Schmitt: Hey everyone, thanks for making time. This is the security follow-up from the demo last week. We’ve got Marcus on from security engineering and Simran who can speak to Private/VPC if we go there.

[00:11] Jordan Patel: Yep, thanks Hannah. We just wanna close the loop on SSO and audit logs and, honestly, data residency. Those are the big three that came out of our internal review.

[00:22] Megan Wu: And SCIM, if you support it, would be helpful.

[00:25] Hannah Schmitt: Great. Maybe Jordan, quick framing — are we trying to clear for a pilot in hosted, or is it automatically VPC?

[00:33] Jordan Patel: For Helio, anything touching PHI eventually ends up VPC. For the pilot, we can do synthetic data but we still need to know the direction. Also procurement wants to understand the contract posture.

[00:46] Hannah Schmitt: Makes sense. We’ll keep this mostly product/security, but we’ll end with the procurement path.

[00:52] Marcus Lin: Hi all. I’ll be direct. We can answer what’s supported today and what’s roadmap vs custom.

[01:00] Samir Qureshi: Cool.

[01:02] Hannah Schmitt: Let’s start with SSO/SAML.

[01:06] Megan Wu: Okay. We’re on Okta. We require SAML SSO for admin console access, ideally enforceable. We also want group-based role mapping. And then SCIM provisioning so we’re not manually creating users.

[01:22] Marcus Lin: Yep. SAML 2.0 is supported for Console access. We’ve tested Okta and Azure AD. You can enforce SSO for the org — meaning password login gets disabled except for break-glass. We do keep one break-glass local admin option, but it’s controlled and logged.

[01:41] Jordan Patel: Break-glass meaning what exactly? Like a local account that bypasses IdP?

[01:46] Marcus Lin: Correct. Typically one or two accounts. They’re protected with strong auth policies and you can require MFA. Access is audited. Some customers also put them behind a vault workflow. We can document recommended controls.

[02:05] Megan Wu: And role mapping via groups?

[02:07] Marcus Lin: We support SAML attributes for role assertions, and we can map IdP groups to Redwood roles. In practice most customers do group → role mapping. The roles are RBAC-style: org admin, billing admin, project admin, project viewer, etc. Simran can add detail on project scoping.

[02:26] Simran Kaur: Yeah, in the Console you can scope access to a project. So for example, your security team can have org-wide read access, while app teams have only their project. API keys can be scoped similarly.

[02:39] Megan Wu: SCIM?

[02:41] Marcus Lin: SCIM is available for enterprise plans. Provisioning and deprovisioning users, and group sync. Sometimes transcription calls it “skim” but yes, SCIM. If you’re doing a pilot, we can start with SAML + manual invite and then layer SCIM when you move to production.

[03:01] Jordan Patel: We’ll need it before production, but pilot is okay.

[03:04] Hannah Schmitt: Great. Next, audit logs.

[03:08] Jordan Patel: We need to know what is logged, how long, and can we export to our SIEM. We use Splunk and sometimes Azure Sentinel depending on the unit.

[03:18] Marcus Lin: Understood. Audit logs cover authentication events (login, SSO assertions, failed logins), admin actions (creating/deleting API keys, changing routing policies, enabling fallbacks, changing retention settings), access control changes (role changes), and deployment actions like model version pin changes or rollout toggles. It’s pretty comprehensive for control plane activities.

[03:45] Samir Qureshi: What about data plane? Like per-request prompts and outputs — is that in audit logs?

[03:52] Marcus Lin: Not in audit logs. That’s request logging/telemetry. Audit logs are “who did what admin thing.” Request logs are a separate stream with configurable retention and sampling.

[04:03] Jordan Patel: Okay, and export?

[04:05] Marcus Lin: You can export audit logs via API. We also have an option for pushing to external storage — like an S3 bucket or a SIEM collector — depending on deployment. For hosted, the standard is API pull and we can do scheduled export jobs. For Private/VPC, it’s easier to route to your logging stack.

[04:28] Megan Wu: Do you support syslog?

[04:30] Marcus Lin: Not natively as syslog today, but we can provide JSON export. Most folks put a small forwarder in place.

[04:38] Jordan Patel: Retention of audit logs?

[04:41] Marcus Lin: Default retention is 90 days on the enterprise tier in hosted, configurable higher as an add-on. For Private/VPC you can set it based on your storage and compliance requirements.

[04:53] Jordan Patel: Can we set it to like 1 year?

[04:55] Hannah Schmitt: Typically yes, that’s common. We’d align it with the package.

[04:58] Marcus Lin: Just to be precise, in hosted: one year is available with extended retention. In Private: you control.

[05:06] Jordan Patel: Got it.

[05:08] Hannah Schmitt: Let’s hit retention controls more broadly. Prompts/outputs, request logs.

[05:13] Jordan Patel: Yeah. We’re sensitive to any storage of prompts and model outputs, even if it’s synthetic at first. Our policy is: minimize, and if stored, strict retention with explicit setting.

[05:26] Marcus Lin: Understood. In hosted, you can configure retention for request/response logs and you can also disable storing prompt/response bodies while still keeping aggregate metrics. Some customers call this “zero retention,” but it’s more like “no content logging.” We still have to process the request to generate output, but we don’t persist the content in logs.

[05:49] Samir Qureshi: When you say metrics, is that token counts and latency?

[05:52] Marcus Lin: Exactly. Things like token usage, latency percentiles, error codes. Not the text itself.

[05:59] Jordan Patel: Is that a per-project setting or org-wide?

[06:02] Simran Kaur: Per project. You can also set org defaults and override at the project level, depending on policy.

[06:10] Megan Wu: And who can change those settings? Only admins?

[06:13] Marcus Lin: Yes, controlled by RBAC. Changes themselves are in audit logs.

[06:19] Jordan Patel: Good.

[06:21] Hannah Schmitt: Data residency. I know this is the thorny one.

[06:25] Jordan Patel: Yeah. We have EU patients, and we’ve been burned by “EU region” meaning compute is EU but logs are US, or support access is global. So we need specifics: where does data go, what about control plane metadata, and what about sub-processors.

[06:45] Simran Kaur: Totally fair. Let me break it down: data plane and control plane. Data plane is the inference traffic — prompts/outputs. In an EU hosted region, inference happens in-region and request content stays in-region under configured logging policy. Control plane includes account metadata and configuration. Depending on setup, some metadata may be stored centrally. For strict residency, we recommend Redwood Private in your VPC, where control plane can be deployed private and you manage data locality more tightly.

[07:20] Samir Qureshi: So if we do hosted EU, are you saying some metadata could still be outside EU?

[07:26] Simran Kaur: Potentially, yes — things like billing identifiers, org settings, but not request content if you set it correctly. We should not over-promise; we can provide the exact architecture doc and what fields are where.

[07:41] Jordan Patel: That’s important. We need to say that clearly to legal.

[07:45] Hannah Schmitt: Yep. We’ll send a written answer with the boundary. We don’t want ambiguity.

[07:50] Megan Wu: What about support access? Can Redwood engineers see prompts in a support case?

[07:56] Marcus Lin: By default, our support can’t just browse customer content. Access is gated and logged. If content logging is disabled, there’s nothing persisted to view. If you enable content logs for debugging, access requires explicit approvals and is audited. For Private/VPC, you can keep logs entirely in your environment.

[08:16] Jordan Patel: Okay. Encryption? At rest and in transit? KMS?

[08:20] Marcus Lin: TLS in transit. Encryption at rest. For hosted, we use cloud KMS (provider-managed with our controls). For Private, we can integrate with your KMS/HSM depending on your setup. Again, we’ll provide specifics in the security packet.

[08:38] Samir Qureshi: Are you SOC 2?

[08:40] Hannah Schmitt: Yes — SOC 2 Type II. We can share under NDA.

[08:44] Jordan Patel: We’ll need that and a subprocessor list.

[08:47] Hannah Schmitt: Understood.

[08:49] (crosstalk)

[08:50] Elena Rossi: Sorry, Elena from procurement. Just jumping in — for us to start paper, we need: standard MSA, DPA, subprocessor list, and the security report. Also we need clarity on data residency because that changes which template we use.

[09:05] Hannah Schmitt: That’s helpful. We can send our standard package. On data residency, if you want strict EU-only for both control and data plane, that pushes toward Private/VPC. If you’re okay with EU data plane and central control plane metadata, hosted EU might work. We’ll document both.

[09:26] Jordan Patel: Right. For pilot we can do synthetic data in hosted, but we need to know that production is VPC.

[09:35] Simran Kaur: One more note: with Private deployment, you can deploy inference and logging within your VPC. The Console can still be used, but it’s within your network boundary. SSO ties into your IdP as usual.

[09:50] Megan Wu: Would that be on Kubernetes? Or you manage it?

[09:53] Simran Kaur: Both options depending on the package. Typically we provide the deployment artifacts and you run it in your environment, and we can offer managed support. But we’ll tailor that in a solutioning call.

[10:10] Jordan Patel: Okay.

[10:12] Hannah Schmitt: I want to make sure we answer your specific questionnaire items. Jordan, do you have the open ones handy?

[10:18] Jordan Patel: Yeah, a few. One: “Does vendor use customer data for training?”

[10:23] Hannah Schmitt: No. We don’t train foundation models on your data.

[10:27] Marcus Lin: And contractually we can commit to that. We use data only to provide the service. Optional improvement programs are opt-in and can be disabled, but by default no training.

[10:39] Jordan Patel: Two: “Data retention minimum.” We want 0 or very low for content.

[10:45] Marcus Lin: You can configure no content logging. For any necessary operational logs, we keep minimal metadata for reliability. We’ll specify what that is.

[10:56] Jordan Patel: Three: “Audit log integrity.” Can logs be tampered with?

[11:01] Marcus Lin: In hosted, logs are stored in append-only systems with access controls. Admins can’t edit historical entries through the UI. We can also export to your SIEM for your own immutability controls.

[11:14] Samir Qureshi: What about API keys rotation? That’s always a concern.

[11:18] Simran Kaur: API keys can be scoped and rotated. You can create multiple keys, disable old ones. We have audit events for key creation and deletion. Also supports short-lived tokens in some contexts.

[11:33] Megan Wu: Does it integrate with our secrets manager?

[11:36] Simran Kaur: We don’t push into your vault automatically, but we provide guidance; most teams store keys in Vault or Secrets Manager. For Private, we can align to your preferred pattern.

[11:49] Jordan Patel: Okay.

[11:51] Hannah Schmitt: I’m going to jump to the data residency nuance again because it’s the one that usually stalls procurement.

[11:57] Elena Rossi: Yes please.

[12:00] Hannah Schmitt: What we can do: for the pilot, use hosted EU region, disable content logging, and keep everything synthetic. Meanwhile we run a parallel evaluation of Private/VPC as the target deployment for production PHI. Procurement can start on the paper using the Private template if that’s the decision.

[12:23] Jordan Patel: That’s probably right. But we need a clear “in hosted EU, these specific things may still be US.” We can live with it for synthetic, but legal needs to know.

[12:35] Simran Kaur: We’ll provide a table: request content, request metadata, account metadata, support telemetry — and the residency for each in hosted EU vs Private.

[12:46] Jordan Patel: Great.

[12:48] Megan Wu: Also: do you support regional failover? If EU is down, do you fail to US?

[12:53] Simran Kaur: Good question. You can configure fallback routing across regions. For strict residency, you’d restrict fallback to EU-only. In demo mode people like to show global failover, but for you we’d lock it down.

[13:08] Jordan Patel: Thank you. That’s exactly the sort of thing we worry about — accidental cross-region.

[13:14] Marcus Lin: And changes to routing policies are audited.

[13:17] Samir Qureshi: Do you have HIPAA BAA?

[13:20] Hannah Schmitt: We can discuss BAA as part of enterprise agreements. Usually requires Private/VPC and specific controls. We can’t promise on this call, but we can route to our legal/security process.

[13:34] Jordan Patel: Okay.

[13:36] (brief pause)

[13:37] Hannah Schmitt: Anything else on SSO before we move to procurement steps?

[13:41] Megan Wu: One more — do you support step-up auth? Like MFA enforced via IdP.

[13:46] Marcus Lin: Via IdP policies, yes. We rely on your IdP for MFA and conditional access. For local break-glass accounts we can enforce MFA too.

[13:58] Megan Wu: Okay.

[14:00] Hannah Schmitt: Procurement steps. Elena, what’s the sequence on your side?

[14:05] Elena Rossi: We can start once we have the packet. We’ll need your standard MSA and DPA. Our legal team will mark it up. We also need the security report and subprocessor list. If we’re going VPC/Private, we may need an exhibit about where it’s hosted and responsibilities.

[14:24] Hannah Schmitt: That’s all standard. We’ll send the standard DPA and a subprocessor list. SOC 2 under NDA — do you already have an NDA in place?

[14:33] Elena Rossi: I think so, but I’ll verify.

[14:36] Hannah Schmitt: Great. Jordan, will you be the point for security questionnaire completion?

[14:41] Jordan Patel: Yes. Megan and Samir will help with the technical bits.

[14:46] Hannah Schmitt: Perfect.

[14:48] Marcus Lin: Also, if you want, we can provide a sample audit log export file so your team can validate fields for Splunk.

[14:56] Jordan Patel: That would be helpful.

[14:58] Samir Qureshi: And I’d like a quick architecture session on Private. Not a sales pitch, just: what runs where, what we operate.

[15:06] Simran Kaur: Happy to. I can do 30 minutes next week.

[15:10] Hannah Schmitt: Okay. Let’s do scheduling offline.

[15:14] Jordan Patel: One last thing — competitors. We’re also looking at Bedrock and Azure OpenAI. They claim “regional” but it’s fuzzy. We need you to be crisp.

[15:24] Hannah Schmitt: Understood. We’ll be crisp. We’ll document what’s in-region, and where the boundaries are.

[15:32] Marcus Lin: And to be explicit, we prefer to put it in writing rather than do hand-wavy statements.

[15:38] Jordan Patel: Great.

[15:40] Hannah Schmitt: I’ll recap and then we can drop. Recap: SAML SSO with Okta supported, enforceable; group-role mapping supported; SCIM available for enterprise. Audit logs cover admin/control-plane events with configurable retention and export. Content logging can be disabled; metrics remain. Data residency: hosted EU keeps inference in-region; some control plane metadata may be centralized; for strict residency and PHI, Private/VPC is recommended. Next steps: we send security packet and written residency table; you kick off legal/procurement; we schedule Private architecture session.

[16:18] Elena Rossi: Works for me.

[16:19] Jordan Patel: Yep.

[16:20] Megan Wu: Thanks.

[16:21] Samir Qureshi: Thanks.

[16:23] Hannah Schmitt: Great, thanks everyone.

(Transcript continues with light scheduling chatter and goodbyes)

[16:30] (end)

title: HelioHealth x Redwood — Security review follow-up (SSO/SAML, audit logs, retention, data residency)
preview: Follow-up security review with HelioHealth after initial demo. Discussion focused on SSO/SAML support, RBAC and SCIM, audit logging scope and export options, data retention controls, and data residency for EU workloads. Redwood clarified hosted vs VPC/Private deployment options and what telemetry is captured by default. Call ended with procurement/legal next steps: DPA + SOC 2, security questionnaire completion, and a short list of redlines for MSA.
text: Follow-up security review with HelioHealth after initial demo. Discussion focused on SSO/SAML support, RBAC and SCIM, audit logging scope and export options, data retention controls, and data residency for EU workloads. Redwood clarified hosted vs VPC/Private deployment options and what telemetry is captured by default. Call ended with procurement/legal next steps: DPA + SOC 2, security questionnaire completion, and a short list of redlines for MSA.

source.title: HelioHealth - Security review (EU residency & support access)
next_steps: Redwood to document audited break-glass support access exception
source.transcript:
MEETING HEADER
Date: 2024-10-04
Time: 11:00 CEST
Duration: 38 min

Topics (auto)
- EU data residency
- Support access and auditing

Transcript
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Today is mainly residency and what our support process looks like.
[00:35] Nina Kovacs: Our requirement is EU only. We also need to know if your support team outside EU can see anything.
[01:20] Ethan Park: Serving and storage can be in EU. For support, we have engineers globally, so the right control is RBAC + break-glass with auditing.
[03:05] Lea Martin: If someone in APAC accesses logs, is that considered data leaving the EU?
[03:22] Evelyn Hart: The audit logs remain stored in-region; the access is remote and logged. We can document the exception explicitly, limited to operational metadata only.
[06:10] Nina Kovacs: We need that written for our file. And please confirm prompts/responses aren't stored.
[06:33] Ethan Park: For your tenant we can keep payload logging off.
...
[36:50] Jordan Blake: Next steps: well send a brief memo and tie it to our exception record.
